verdicts.t1: Strong fit: importer/distributor with reselling channel, in-product, no blocking pattern.
verdicts.t2: Real opportunity but smaller, slower, or partial. Send but expect lower close rate.
verdicts.t3: Acknowledged but not a priority. Long sales cycle, narrow product surface, or producer with only minor resale gap.

patterns.confirmed-good-fit: Client explicitly approved (✅). The ideal profile has TWO axes, both with verbatim evidence: (A) IMPORTS / buys container volume rather than producing everything in-house, and (B) sells under its OWN brand(s). t1 requires evidence of both. A manufacturer-distributor with real import/distribution motion and its own brand can still be t1 (INTERMAS, Naue, Nice-Pak, MONGE, H-Pack — all client-confirmed), but never assert importing without a quoted claim from the website or an external source (customs data, 'official importer of...', 'we import from...').
patterns.pure-manufacturer-no-import: Factory that produces everything and imports nothing ('fábrica full, no importan nada'). Product overlap with Capricorn's catalog does NOT help — selling to them means competing with their own production ('estaría entrando a competirle'). Client iteration-3: TATAY, Geo&Tex 2000, Metzer, A.A. Politiv, Sadovsky — 'Manufacturer, sin muchas pistas de ser importador de nada... waste of time'. Reject.
patterns.adjacent-category-mismatch: The company's CORE business is an adjacent category, not a Capricorn vertical — transit/industrial packaging instead of foodservice disposables (RAJAPACK), irrigation motors instead of irrigation consumables (NETTUNO), coco substrate instead of agricultural plastics (Pelemix), or the matching division is a minor side line so volume would be small (IL TELONE). Keyword co-occurrence is not product fit; judge the core business. Reject (t3 if some real but secondary surface exists).
patterns.food-distributor-not-disposables: Distributes FOOD to foodservice operators, not foodservice disposables ('Empresa de comida, no de disposables de foodservice' — Thomas Ridley, Tok Food). Disposables as a side category in a food catalog does not qualify. Reject.
patterns.group-subsidiary-local-mismatch: Local subsidiary of a global group whose relevance differs per country. Judge the LOCAL entity's behavior: Bunzl is Capricorn's client in Chile, but Bunzl UK buys from local importers and sells third-party brands — 'No relevante en UK'. Never qualify a company off the global group's profile. Reject when the local entity fails the importer/own-brand test.
patterns.third-party-distributor-product-fit: Distributor with real product fit but NO own brand — sells third-party (niche, non-global) brands. Client on Harpo: 'Product fit, pero no tienen private label... No son prioridad pero podría ser interesante.' Deliver as t3, clearly flagged 'third-party brand reseller — low priority'. If the brands carried are GLOBAL brands (Eukanuba/Pedigree/Hills class), use no-own-brand-reseller (reject) instead.
patterns.producer-who-also-imports: Manufacturer with VERIFIED import/distribution motion (quoted claim or customs evidence) who could resell complementary Capricorn SKUs. t2 — promote to t1 only with strong volume evidence plus own brand (the judge decides, citing evidence).
patterns.volume-dependent-tier: Confirmed fit where t1 vs t2 hinges on the volume of Capricorn-relevant SKUs they move (Green-tech: 'habría que ver si son T1 o T2, dependiendo del volumen'). Without volume evidence, default t2.
patterns.claimed-import-without-evidence: Import status unverified — no real claim found ('Claude dice que importan pq su range es muy grande, pero sin un claim real' — Textil Villa de Pego; 'No sé... si será importador' — EQUIP-AGRO). A wide product range is NOT import evidence. Cap at t2 + needs_human_check naming the missing evidence.
patterns.dead-website: Company website does not resolve (Geomembrane Bresciani). Caught deterministically by the dead-site gate; never deliver. Reject.
patterns.cosmetics-pure-producer-slow-cycle: Cosmetics manufacturer that produces and sells its own brand. Capricorn can only offer packaging or complements, and these clients are slow to close. Mark t3 unless the company is also a manufacturer+importer with active third-party sourcing.
patterns.eu-made-proud-producer: Producer that markets itself heavily as locally manufactured (e.g. 'EU Made', 'Made in Spain'). Asia-sourced product is brand-incompatible. t3 at best.
patterns.dtc-only-own-brand: Sells only what it produces, often as part of brand identity (Lush). No reseller motion exists. Reject.
patterns.out-of-product-fragrance: Fragrance / scent house. Capricorn does not sell fragrances. Reject.
patterns.producer-non-priority: Single-line factory (e.g. paper cups). Has a buying budget but rarely buys from third parties, and not the client's priority. t3.
patterns.has-asia-sourcing-already: Has an in-house Asia sourcing team (Hong Kong/Shanghai office, listed Asia procurement). Not a no-go but much harder to land. Downgrade to t2.
patterns.ingredient-supplier-not-finished-goods: Sells cosmetic raw materials or ingredients, not finished products. Capricorn imports finished SKUs, so the supply chain doesn't overlap. Reject.
patterns.producer-with-resale-gap: Producer with their own customer base who COULD resell complementary Capricorn SKUs to fill a gap in their catalogue, but the import/distribute motion is NOT explicit on the website. Use this when the upside is real but inferred, not stated. Reserve t1 (confirmed-good-fit) for cases where the company explicitly imports, distributes, or wholesales today. t2.
patterns.agrochemicals-wrong-supply-chain: Agrochemicals, biostimulants, fertilizer chemistry, seeds — chemistry supply chain. Capricorn sells physical agricultural inputs (films, nets, hoses, irrigation, grow bags). No overlap. Reject.
patterns.biological-pest-control-not-physical-inp: Biological / biocontrol / pest-control company. Same exclusion logic as agrochemicals — Capricorn sells physical inputs. Reject.
patterns.animal-feed-not-pet-food: Livestock / cattle feed, not dog/cat food. Different industry, different buyers. Reject.
patterns.construction-chemicals-wrong-supply-chai: Construction chemistry (adhesives, sealants, coatings). Not membranes/geotextiles in the physical-input sense. Reject.
patterns.no-wholesaler-channel: Brand that sells only through its own DTC channels (no wholesaler/distributor program). Volume per deal is smaller. Downgrade to t2.
patterns.ultra-premium-anti-commodity: Ultra-premium brand explicitly positioned as anti-commodity. Buys expensive, low-volume specialty inputs. Reject.
patterns.native-ingredient-only: Brand built around a native ingredient (Dead Sea minerals, regional botanicals). Won't import a competing source. At most: packaging only. t3.
patterns.no-own-brand-reseller: Has no own private-label line — its business model is distributing OTHER manufacturers' brands ('we represent X, Y, Z brands'; 'authorized distributor of...'; 'official importer of...'). Capricorn private-label products cannot slot into a no-own-brand reseller because there is no brand to put them under and the catalog is built on third-party brand contracts. Client iteration-3, verbatim: 'ALGO QUE CALIFICA MUCHO A UNA EMPRESA, ES QUE VENDA SUS MARCAS PROPIAS. Si vende marcas de terceros, o marcas globales tiende a no ser relevante (por ejemplo, un distribuidor de comida de mascotas que venda Eukanuba, Pedigree y Hills).' Anchors: Pedigree Wholesale, DIBOSCH, Famobra. This is DIFFERENT from a manufacturer-distributor with its own brand who also distributes — that's a positive signal. For a niche-brand distributor with real product fit, use third-party-distributor-product-fit (t3) instead. Reject.
patterns.direct-competitor: ONLY use when (a) the company is on the named-competitor list below, or (b) the company is a pure private-label competitor — i.e. it manufactures the SAME finished SKUs AND sells them through the SAME channels in the SAME geography Capricorn already serves, with no visible procurement of complementary inputs. A regional manufacturer that makes one product line Capricorn also sells is NOT automatically a competitor — they usually buy complementary SKUs. Examples of TRUE competitors named by the client: N.R. Spuntech (wipes in Israel). When in doubt, prefer confirmed-good-fit or producer-with-resale-gap over direct-competitor.
patterns.medical-clinical-not-consumer: Medical / clinical-grade products rather than consumer cosmetics. Different supply chain, different buyers. Reject.
patterns.not-volume-importer: Doesn't look like a typical container-volume importer. Smaller scale or unusual buying motion. t3.

labels.client_comment: Más o menos. Como es productor de cosméticos, en lo que puedo aportar en su core business es vendiéndoles packaging. La otra sería venderles complementos. Pero estos en general son clientes que toman mucho tiempo, y hasta que gano buenos deals habrá que tener paciencia. Si fuera manufacturer e importador sería muy bueno.
✅. La compradora con la que estoy en contacto es Miriam Gasulla. Ella es relevante también por ejemplo.

Más o menos. Como es productor de cosméticos, en lo que puedo aportar en su core business es vendiéndoles packaging. La otra sería venderles complementos. Pero estos en general son clientes que toman mucho tiempo, y hasta que gano buenos deals habrá que tener paciencia. Si fuera manufacturer e importador sería muy bueno.
Productor y es muy "EU Made" Proud. Podría ofrecérseles packaging, pero es la misma historia que con Freshly Cosmetics y Mesoestetic. No son empresas para descartar del todo, pero no son T1.
NO RELEVANTE. Aparentemente solo venden lo que producen y es parte de su identidad de marca. Pérdida de tiempo ir a venderles.
NO RELEVANTE. No vendemos fragancias.
✅. Eso sí, considerar que son disposables de fiesta más que el clásico foodservice disposables.
Este más o menos, ahí califiqué mal yo. Son fábrica de vasos de papel. Puede ser interesante, pero es más difícil que me compren y no son mi prioridad.

No va en T1. Tiene productos que le puedo vender, pero no mucha variedad. Esto lo sé yo, pero tal vez puede filtrarse también, pero tienen un equipo de sourcing en Hong Kong, que no es un no-go, pero tiende a ser más difícil 'to land this client'.
NO RELEVANTE. Es un cosmetics ingredient supplier. Lo que busco sería a quienes venderles desde Asia productos cosméticos terminados.

✅. Producen, pero hay un gap de productos que no importan que pueden explotar con Capricorn y vender a sus clientes existentes. No creo que sean T1 pero se ven como una empresa para venderles productos complementarios.
Empresa más dirigida a los químicos agrícolas, es un supply chain muy distinto al mío. NO RELEVANTE.
Empresa más dirigida a los químicos agrícolas, es un supply chain muy distinto al mío. NO RELEVANTE.
NO RELEVANTE. Comida de ganado, no de perros y gatos. Además creo que el HQ está en Tailandia.
Es empresa de químicos de construcción. NO RELEVANTE.

✅. Bien, pero no va en T1, pq parece ser que no venden a wholesalers, sino que sólo por sus propios medios, entonces imagino que su volumen no es tan grande como un importador/distribuidor del T1. Pero sigue siendo un muy buen target.
NO RELEVANTE. Es muy anti-commodity, están posicionados como ultra-premium y compran cosas caras.
No va en T1. Producen y venden productos solo que estén hechos con prods del mar muerto. Entonces no importarán productos de China. Solo envases imagino.
NO RELEVANTE. No tienen su marca propia, venden marcas existentes.
NO RELEVANTE. Son mi competencia, yo vendería los mismos productos. De hecho, sacaría los wipes en Israel como target.
Son más clínicos que cosméticos generales. NO RELEVANTE.
NO RELEVANTE. Lo que vende Capricorn son productos físicos para agricultura, esta empresa va más por el lado biológico y de pest control. No hay overlap con los productos nuestros.
No es irrelevante, pero no va en el T1, viene harto más abajo. Parece que las compró una empresa más grande el año pasado, lo que puede cambiar todo. Pero no parece ser un typical volumen importer.

global_notes.agriculture_targeting: Capricorn sells physical agricultural inputs (films, nets, hoses, irrigation, grow bags). Discovery keywords for agriculture must be the products themselves — agrotextiles, agricultural plastics, mulch film, anti-hail nets, shade nets, olive nets, drip irrigation, greenhouse supplies, grow bags, layflat hose — and the targets must be importers / distributors / wholesalers of those physical products. Agrochemicals, biostimulants, biocontrol, seeds, and animal/cattle nutrition are out of scope.
global_notes.israel_wipes_excluded: Israel is out of scope for the Wipes vertical — direct competitive overlap with N.R. Spuntech.
global_notes.cosmetics_priority: Pure-producer cosmetics brands (DTC, EU-made-proud, ultra-premium, ingredient suppliers, contract-only, clinical) are low priority. Cosmetics targets should be importers/distributors with a wholesale channel, OR producers that also openly import third-party finished goods to resell.
global_notes.manufacturers_rule: REVISED after iteration 3 (supersedes the old 'manufacturers are prospects' note). Capricorn's best customers IMPORT container volume and sell OWN brands — 'LO MEJOR SON LOS IMPORTADORES CON LOS QUE PUEDA VENDER VOLUMEN' (client, iteration 3). A manufacturer is a prospect ONLY with verified import/distribution motion: quoted claim or customs evidence → producer-who-also-imports (t2; t1 only with own brand + volume evidence — the iteration-1 confirms INTERMAS/Naue/MONGE/Nice-Pak all had real motion). A pure manufacturer with no import evidence is a REJECT ('waste of time'), regardless of product overlap. Never infer importing from a wide product range.
global_notes.own_brand_rule: Client, iteration 3, verbatim: 'ALGO QUE CALIFICA MUCHO A UNA EMPRESA, ES QUE VENDA SUS MARCAS PROPIAS.' Own brand(s) = strong qualifier. Third-party niche brands with product fit = t3, low priority. Global brands (Eukanuba/Pedigree/Hills class) = reject.
global_notes.israel_targeting: Israel has a strong agri-tech MANUFACTURING tradition — a poor hunting ground for Capricorn's ICP (Pelemix, Politiv, Metzer, Timac, Syngenta, BioBee all failed). The target segment is the smaller group of IMPORTERS/distributors bringing physical goods into Israel from Asia. Discovery must use importer-intent terms ('importer', 'official importer', 'יבואן', 'יבוא') combined with product categories (agricultural plastics, films, irrigation accessories), not generic agriculture terms. An Israeli agriculture company with manufacturer=yes and no import evidence → reject by default. Deliver fewer, higher-confidence Israeli leads rather than backfilling with manufacturers.
global_notes.local_entity_rule: For subsidiaries of global groups, evaluate the LOCAL entity in the target country, not the group (Bunzl: client in Chile, reject in UK).

known_rejects.reason: Client confirms: no own private-label, distributes other brands only — Capricorn private-label can't slot in. Reject even though firmographics show manufacturer + distribution network.
Client iteration-3: already a Capricorn client in Chile; the UK entity buys from local importers and sells third-party brands — 'No relevante en UK'.

tier_calibration_guidance: Bias toward t3 (not reject) when a thin sales surface exists. Examples: native-ingredient brands (AHAVA, KORRES) still buy packaging and complementary items; producer-non-priority (Benders) buys low volume but still buys; third-party NICHE-brand distributors with product fit (Harpo). Only choose 'reject' when the supply chain is fundamentally incompatible (pure manufacturers with no import motion, global-brand distributors, food distributors, agrochemicals, ingredient suppliers, biocontrol, competitors, DTC-only with no procurement, ultra-premium anti-commodity, adjacent-category core business). t1 is reserved for evidence-backed importer + own-brand companies; when import or own-brand status is unknown, cap at t2 and name the missing evidence. When the client has labelled an analogous case, match that tier, do not be harsher.
